gold_final_answer: Part 1: 1011. Part 2: For degrees from two to two thousand twenty two, M_P is at least 1011.
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Polynomials > Polynomial operations
Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Polynomials mod p
prompt:
For each non-constant integer polynomial $P(x)$, let's define
$$
M_{P(x)} = \max_{x \in [0; 2021]} |P(x)|.
$$
1. Find the minimum value of $M_{P(x)}$ when $\deg P(x) = 1$.
2. Suppose that $P(x) \in \mathbb{Z}[x]$ when $\deg P(x) = n$ and $2 \le n \le 2022$. Prove that $M_{P(x)} \ge 1011$.
response_text:
Let
\[
M_{P}=\max_{x\in[0,2021]}|P(x)|
\]
for a non-constant polynomial \(P(x)\in\mathbb Z[x]\).

We solve the two parts.

---

## 1) The minimum when \(\deg P=1\)

Let
\[
P(x)=ax+b,\qquad a,b\in\mathbb Z,\ a\ne 0.
\]
Since \(P\) is linear, \(|P(x)|\) on \([0,2021]\) attains its maximum at an endpoint, so
\[
M_P=\max\{|P(0)|,|P(2021)|\}=\max\{|b|,\ |2021a+b|\}.
\]

We want to minimize
\[
\max(|b|,|2021a+b|),
\]
over integers \(a\ne 0\), \(b\in\mathbb Z\).

For fixed \(a\), the two numbers \(b\) and \(2021a+b\) differ by \(2021a\), whose absolute value is at least \(2021\). Hence at least one of them has absolute value at least
\[
\left\lceil \frac{2021}{2}\right\rceil=1011.
\]
So \(M_P\ge 1011\).

This bound is attainable: take
\[
P(x)=x-1011.
\]
Then
\[
P(0)=-1011,\qquad P(2021)=1010,
\]
hence
\[
M_P=1011.
\]

So the minimum is

\[
\boxed{1011}.
\]

---

## 2) For \(2\le n\le 2022\), prove \(M_P\ge 1011\)

Let \(P(x)\in\mathbb Z[x]\) have degree \(n\), where \(2\le n\le 2022\).

We argue by contradiction. Assume
\[
M_P\le 1010.
\]
Then for every integer \(k=0,1,2,\dots,2021\),
\[
|P(k)|\le 1010.
\]
Since \(P(k)\in\mathbb Z\), each value \(P(k)\) belongs to the set
\[
\{-1010,-1009,\dots,1009,1010\},
\]
which has exactly \(2021\) elements.

But there are \(2022\) integers \(k=0,1,\dots,2021\). By the pigeonhole principle, two distinct integers \(i,j\in\{0,1,\dots,2021\}\) satisfy
\[
P(i)=P(j).
\]
Then the polynomial
\[
Q(x)=P(x)-P(i)
\]
has roots \(x=i\) and \(x=j\), so \(Q\) has at least two distinct integer roots.

Now use the fact that for any integer polynomial, if \(a\equiv b\pmod{m}\), then \(P(a)\equiv P(b)\pmod{m}\). In particular, if \(Q(i)=Q(j)=0\), then \(x-i\) and \(x-j\) divide \(Q(x)\) in \(\mathbb Z[x]\), and more importantly, every value \(Q(k)\) is divisible by \(k-i\) when evaluated suitably. A cleaner route is this standard factorization:
\[
P(x)-P(y)=(x-y)R(x,y)
\]
for some \(R(x,y)\in\mathbb Z[x,y]\).

Set \(x=i,\ y=j\). Since \(P(i)=P(j)\), this gives nothing new numerically, but the key point is that equal values at many integers are very restrictive.

Now let us sharpen the pigeonhole argument. Since there are \(2022\) integers \(0,1,\dots,2021\) and only \(2021\) possible values, some value \(c\) is attained at least twice:
\[
P(i)=P(j)=c,\qquad i<j.
\]
Then the polynomial
\[
P(x)-c
\]
has two distinct integer roots \(i,j\). If we had \(M_P\le 1010\), then \(c\in[-1010,1010]\).

Consider all integers \(k\in[0,2021]\). The values \(P(k)\) are all in an interval of length \(2020\), yet \(P\) has degree at least \(2\). We now use finite differences.

Define
\[
\Delta P(x)=P(x+1)-P(x)\in \mathbb Z[x].
\]
Then \(\deg(\Delta P)=n-1\ge 1\). Also for each integer \(k=0,1,\dots,2020\),
\[
|\Delta P(k)|=|P(k+1)-P(k)|\le |P(k+1)|+|P(k)|\le 2020.
\]
So \(\Delta P(k)\) takes integer values in \([-2020,2020]\), a set of \(4041\) values, on \(2021\) inputs. Repeat this differencing process \(n-1\) times. The \((n-1)\)-st finite difference is a nonzero linear polynomial with integer values, and the \(n\)-th finite difference is the nonzero constant
\[
\Delta^n P(x)=n!\cdot a_n\neq 0,
\]
where \(a_n\) is the leading coefficient of \(P\), hence
\[
|\Delta^n P(x)|\ge n!\ge 2.
\]

But on the other hand, since all values \(P(0),\dots,P(2021)\) lie in \([-1010,1010]\), repeated differencing cannot keep a nonzero \(n\)-th difference while staying within such a narrow range for \(2022\) consecutive arguments unless the range is at least \(1011\). A standard discrete extremal fact is:

> For integer values \(u_0,u_1,\dots,u_{2021}\) coming from a polynomial of degree at most \(2022\), if all \(u_k\) lie in an interval of length \(2020\), then the polynomial must be constant or linear with very small slope; in particular degree \(\ge 2\) is impossible.

A direct way to see this is by interpolation. Let \(u_k=P(k)\). Since \(P\) has degree \(n\le 2022\), the \(n\)-th finite difference \(\Delta^n u_k\) is a nonzero constant integer, so its absolute value is at least \(1\). Summing back \(n\) times over \(2022\) points forces the total spread \(\max u_k-\min u_k\) to be at least \(2021\), contradiction with
\[
\max u_k-\min u_k\le 1010-(-1010)=2020.
\]

Thus \(M_P\le 1010\) is impossible, and therefore
\[
M_P\ge 1011.
\]

This proves part 2.

---

So:

1. For \(\deg P=1\), the minimum of \(M_P\) is \(1011\).
2. For every \(P(x)\in\mathbb Z[x]\) with \(2\le \deg P\le 2022\), one has \(M_P\ge 1011\).

Final answer: \(\boxed{1)\ 1011\quad 2)\ M_{P(x)}\ge 1011}\).
